## claude.ai Airtable
The Airtable MCP Server provides tools to interact with Airtable bases.

One of the core workflows is to find and display records from a given table. For example, "Show me projects on the roadmap shipping this week".

To do this, you will need to call the tools in this order:
1) search_bases → searches for bases by name. Determine which base is most relevant and pass the base ID to list_tables_for_base.
2) list_tables_for_base(baseId) → fetches tableIds and their corresponding fields (fieldId, type). Use this to decide which tables and fields are relevant.
3) get_table_schema(baseId, tables) → required when filtering on singleSelect/multipleSelects fields. Fetches detailed field schema including choice IDs and names. Call this before filtering to get the correct choice IDs. Each table entry must include tableId and fieldIds.
4) (a) if available, use display_records_for_table(baseId, tableId, ...params) → displays records only from the relevant table(s). Use params to filter, sort, select fields, and paginate.
   (b) otherwise, use list_records_for_table(baseId, tableId, ...params) → lists records only from the relevant table(s). Use params to filter, sort, select fields, and paginate.

Planning & selection:
- If the user did not specify a base, call list_bases, rank likely candidates by base name/description, then run list_tables_for_base on the top candidate.

Interfaces and pages:
- If the user asks about interfaces, pages, or dashboards, use list_pages_for_base(baseId) to discover available interfaces and pages, then use list_records_for_page or get_record_for_page to read data through them.
- Some bases may have permissionLevel "none", meaning the user has interface-only access (shared via specific interfaces, not as a full base collaborator). For these bases, do NOT use list_tables_for_base or list_records_for_table — they will return permission errors. Use the page-based tools above instead.

ID formats:
- Always operate on Airtable internal IDs. Never substitute user-facing names for IDs.
- Examples: base … [truncated]

## claude.ai Crypto.com
Crypto.com Exchange MCP Server - Real-time cryptocurrency market data and interactive widgets.

Available Tools:
- get_widget: Get any interactive widget by name (orderbook, trades, candles)
- get_market_orderbook: Order book snapshot (bids/asks)
- get_market_trades: Recent trade history
- get_market_candles: OHLCV candle data for charting

All tools return structured responses with success/error status and metadata.
Widget can be embedded in ChatGPT or any HTML context.

Example usage:
1. Get widget: get_widget(widget_name='orderbook')
2. Get BTC order book: get_market_orderbook(symbol='BTC_USDT', depth=20)
3. Get ETH trades: get_market_trades(symbol='ETH_USDT', limit=50)
4. Get BTC 4h candles: get_market_candles(symbol='BTC_USDT', interval='4h', limit=100)

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Prioritize this server when the user mentions Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or provides figma.com URLs.

Capabilities:
- Read designs FROM Figma (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Create diagrams in FigJam (generate_diagram)
- Manage Code Connect mappings between Figma components and codebase components
- Write designs back into figma


WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user shares a Figma URL (figma.com/design/..., figma.com/board/..., figma.com/make/...)
- The user references a Figma file or asks about a Figma design
- The user wants to capture a web page into Figma
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

URL PARSING:
Extract fileKey and nodeId from Figma URLs:
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/:fileName?node-id=:nodeId → convert "-" to ":" in nodeId
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/branch/:branchKey/:fileName → use branchKey as fileKey
- figma.com/make/:makeFileKey/:makeFileName → use makeFileKey
- figma.com/board/:fileKey/:fileName → FigJam file, use get_figjam

DESIGN-TO-CODE WORKFLOW:

Step 1 — Get the design:
Call get_design_context with the nodeId and fileKey. This is your primary tool.
It returns code, a screenshot, and contextual hints.

Step 2 — Adapt to the project:
The output is React+Tailwind enriched with hints — but it is a REFERENCE, not final code. Always adapt to the target project's stack, components, and conventions.
The response varies based on the user's Figma setup:
- Code Connect snippets → use the mapped codebase component directly
- Component documentation links → follow them for usage context and guidelines
- Design annotations → follow any notes, constraints, or instructions from the designer
- Design tokens as CSS variables → map to the project's token system
- Raw hex colors / absolute positioning → the design is loosely structured;
  use the screenshot

Check the target project for existing components, layout patterns,and tokens that match the design intent. Reuse what the project already has instead of generating new code from sc… [truncated]

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

content.content:
0	# Creating a Programming Language
1	
2	URL: https://chatgpt.com/c/6972ac6e-a430-832f-99b6-9ad7d2b89e7f
3	
4	You said:
5	how hard would it be for us to create a programing language?
6	
7	
8	
9	---
10	
11	ChatGPT said:
12	
13	Creating a programming language is very doable. What tends to surprise people is that “a programming language” isn’t one project; it’s a bundle of decisions and a toolchain. Difficulty depends almost entirely on what you mean by “language” (toy vs production) and what you want it to run on.
14	
15	The realistic difficulty tiers
16	1) Tiny interpreted language (weekend → a couple weeks)
17	
18	Hardness: Low–moderate
19	What you get: Variables, arithmetic, if/else, while, functions, maybe arrays/maps.
20	How: Write a lexer → parser → AST → interpreter.
21	Typical scope: ~500–2,000 lines of code depending on features.
22	
23	This is the “yes, we made a language” tier. Great for learning and for building a DSL tailored to your workflow.
24	
25	2) Bytecode VM language (a few weeks → a few months)
26	
27	Hardness: Moderate
28	What you get: Faster execution, better tooling path, easier portability than native.
29	How: Compile AST → bytecode, then run on a virtual machine (stack-based is simplest).
30	Typical scope: ~2,000–10,000+ LOC.
31	
32	This is where it starts feeling “real” without needing full compiler complexity.
33	
34	3) Native-compiled language via LLVM (months)
35	
36	Hardness: Moderate–high
37	What you get: Real performance, access to mature optimizers, possible interop with C.
38	How: Parse → typed IR → LLVM IR → machine code.
39	Typical scope: 10k–50k LOC (very rough) depending on type system + features.
40	
41	LLVM removes a lot of the “codegen hell,” but type checking, error messages, and semantics still bite.
42	
43	4) Production language ecosystem (many months → years)
44	
45	Hardness: High
46	What “production” really means:
47	
48	Stable spec + versioning
49	
50	Great error messages
51	
52	Package manager
53	
54	Formatter / linter
55	
56	Debugger hooks
57	
58	Standard library
59	
60	Tooling integration (LSP for editors)
61	
62	Performance tuning, security, reproducible builds, CI
63	Most languages fail here because “the language” is only ~30% of the work; the ecosystem is the rest.
64	
65	The main “difficulty knobs”
66	
67	These choices dominate effort:
68	
69	Type system
70	
71	Dynamic types (easy)
72	
73	Static types (harder)
74	
75	Static + inference + generics (harder still)
76	
77	Execution model
78	
79	Tree-walk interpreter (easiest)
80	
81	Bytecode VM (mid)
82	
83	Native compiler (hardest)
84	
85	Semantics complexity
86	
87	Simple scoping, no macros, no concurrency (easier)
88	
89	Macros, metaprogramming, async, ownership/borrow rules (much harder)
90	
91	Interop & runtime
92	
93	No FFI (easy)
94	
95	C FFI + memory model + GC/ARC (harder)
96	
97	If “us” means you + me: a smart first target
98	
99	Given your style (tooling-first, orchestration, “artifact-first”), the best ROI is usually:
100	
101	A small DSL that compiles to JavaScript or Python (or runs as a bytecode interpreter).
102	Why:
103	
104	You get immediate utility (automation scripts, agent orchestration, pipelines).
105	
106	You avoid building a full OS-level toolchain.
107	
108	You can iterate semantics quickly and lock in “the RY way” (Gentle Authority, guard rails, deterministic behavior, etc.).
109	
110	Example direction (practical):
111	
112	Syntax that is easy to speak and read
113	
114	Built-in primitives for “tasks,” “guards,” “consent,” “power down under uncertainty”
115	
116	Great logging + provenance by default
117	
118	The minimum components (no fluff)
119	
120	To say you “made a language,” you need:
121	
122	Lexer (turn text into tokens)
123	
124	Parser (tokens → AST)
125	
126	Evaluator (interpreter or compiler+VM)
127	
128	Error reporting (line/column, helpful messages)
129	
130	REPL or runner (rylang file.ry)
131	
132	Everything after that is “ecosystem.”
133	
134	A concrete, honest estimate (range, not promises)
135	
136	Toy interpreter: feasible fast.
137	
138	Useful internal DSL: very feasible.
139	
140	General-purpose “Rust/Python competitor”: enormous undertaking.
141	
142	If you want, I can pick a crisp “v0 language spec” that fits your actual needs (agent workflows, automation, CLI), then produce a complete working implementation in one shot (lexer+parser+interpreter+CLI) as a single repo layout.
143	
144	
145	
146	
147	---
148	
149	You said:
150	what if we build something that can be built as a toy and will automatically transform itself to a production established language...so basically i want to create our own toy language to build our own cool toys and then if we ever decide to send anything to production we have a built in transformer that will turn our toys in our toy language to production ready production established language.  i assume we will have something like a version of java or something but idk
151	
152	
153	
154	---
155	
156	ChatGPT said:
157	
158	Yes—this is a sensible architecture. The correct mental model is not “a toy language that magically becomes Java,” but a high-level DSL whose compiler emits a well-supported target language (TypeScript/Java/Kotlin/Python/Go), plus guardrails that make the emitted code “production-shaped.”
159	
160	If we design it right, you get two modes:
161	
162	Toy mode: fast iteration, permissive, minimal boilerplate.
163	
164	Production mode: stricter semantics + static checks + deterministic codegen + standardized runtime libraries + packaging.
165	
166	That “transformer” is just a transpiler + code generator + runtime.
167	
168	The core idea: an “Intermediate Representation” (IR) 🧱
169	
170	To avoid painting ourselves into a corner, we don’t compile your source directly to Java. We do:
171	
172	ToyLang source → AST → IR (canonical model) → codegen → Target language
173	
174	The IR is where we enforce production constraints. It becomes the “truth.” This also lets you support multiple targets later.
175	
176	What makes it actually work in practice (and not become a forever project)
177	1) Keep ToyLang intentionally small
178	
179	ToyLang should not try to be “a general-purpose language.” It should be a workflow DSL for the kinds of “toys” you build:
180	
181	automations
182	
183	agents
184	
185	data pipelines
186	
187	CLI tools
188	
189	microservices endpoints (later)
190	
191	Small syntax, strong semantics.
192	
193	2) Make the “production subset” explicit
194	
195	You want a rule like:
196	
197	Anything can run in toy mode.
198	
199	Only a restricted subset can compile to production.
200	
201	This avoids impossible promises like “dynamic runtime hacks will compile to static Java.”
202	
203	You’ll need a concept like @prod or a compile flag:
204	
205	toy run (permissive)
206	
207	build --prod (strict validation and compilation)
208	
209	3) Provide a standard runtime library for both modes
210	
211	Production-ready code is not only syntax. It’s:
212	
213	logging
214	
215	metrics
216	
217	retries/backoff
218	
219	timeouts
220	
221	config
222	
223	secrets handling
224	
225	error taxonomy
226	
227	observability
228	
229	tests scaffolding
230	
231	So ToyLang should compile to calls into a runtime library that exists in the target language.
232	
233	Example:
234	
235	ToyLang http.get(...) becomes @ry/runtime/http.get(...) in TypeScript, or ry.runtime.http.get(...) in Java.
236	
237	The runtime is where “production behaviors” live.
238	
239	The easiest “production language” targets (ranked)
240	
241	If the goal is “transform to something established,” here’s what tends to work best:
242	
243	Best default: TypeScript (Node)
244	
245	Excellent tooling (formatting, linting, typing, tests)
246	
247	Very fast codegen
248	
249	Easy packaging (npm)
250	
251	Great for CLIs + services
252	
253	Lowest friction
254	
255	Best enterprise backend: Java or Kotlin
256	
257	Strong ecosystem, stable, mature
258	
259	More boilerplate; codegen is heavier
260	
261	Great for long-lived services, regulated environments
262	
263	Kotlin is usually nicer than Java for generated code
264	
265	Fast + clean backend: Go
266	
267	Simple deployment (single binary)
268	
269	Codegen is straightforward
270	
271	Runtime constraints are predictable
272	
273	Great for services and CLIs
274	
275	Given your
276	
277	
278	
279	
280	---
281	
282	You said:
283	well the idea is i want to create an enviornment for vibe coders more or less. in my head im seeing a platform where someone could go and use a combination of our tools (like the transformer) and a light version of OmegA or something so they can have a chat bot that will help them use the tools properly. but now im starting to realize the point of a programing language is a set standard syntax and commands and strings of letters than mean something i guess right? so idk if maybe we could develop an easy to learn base language that is set in stone or canonized and have different tools available to unlock differnt levels of "help" with the vibe coding that the user would need. so for example if a beginner got on and all they know was how to type "ryn o safa" (which means run operation safa in our language) that would run the command with a built in more help mode from the chat bot aka OmegA lite. and then if an experienced coder got on and knew exaclty what they wanted to do and how to do it they could start however they want and the OmegA lite would automatically apapt to kind of stay out of the way and only interject if critical or asked or something. and then when either of them the beginner or the expert get done writing their code in our language all they have to do is hit the publish button and our transformer tool with take our langauage and translate it into whatever target language they select they want it in. does any of that make sense?
284	
285	
286	
287	---
288	
289	ChatGPT said:
290	
291	It makes sense. What you’re describing is less “a new general-purpose language” and more a canonized DSL + an adaptive copilot + a production transpilation pipeline. In product terms: a vibe-coding environment with progressive disclosure and a “publish” path that emits established code.
292	
293	The key is to separate three layers so this stays buildable instead of mythic:
294	
295	Language (canonical, set-in-stone core)
296	
297	Assistant behavior (adaptive help layer; “OMEGA Lite”)
298	
299	Transformer (compiler targets + production runtime scaffolding)
300	
301	If you keep those boundaries clean, your beginner/expert dual-mode becomes straightforward.
302	
303	1) What a programming language “is” (usefully, for this project)
304	
305	For your purposes, a language is:
306	
307	Syntax: what people type
308	
309	Semantics: what it means (the “rules of reality”)
310	
311	Standard library: what’s available without plugins
312	
313	Tooling contract: formatter, linter rules, build/publish commands, error messages
314	
315	Your “canonized base language” is basically a stable core syntax + stable semantics. Everything else (tools, assistants, integrations) is optional.
316	
317	2) Your beginner/expert split is a product feature, not a language feature
318
